title: Interband Berry connection measurement in the optical honeycomb lattice
authors: Shao-Wen Chang, Malte N. Schwarz, Erin G. Moloney, Ke Lin, Dan M. Stamper-Kurn
abstract: The geometry of Bloch bands affects many physical properties of crystalline solids and other spatially periodic systems. Direct experimental determination of such geometry is an active area of research. In this work, we focus on the fundamental connection between optical excitations and the relative geometry of pairs of Bloch bands, as characterized by the interband Berry connection. We simulate the response of electrons in solids to optical excitation by the response of ultracold fermionic atoms in optical lattices to periodic modulation of the lattice position. The strength of resonant excitation between bands, measured at each quasimomentum and for various lattice-shaking polarizations, directly maps out the interband Berry connection. We apply this method to the optical honeycomb lattice, driving excitations between the ground $n=1$ band and the excited $n'=\{2,3,4\}$ bands. We observe transparency lines of quasimomenta at which the response to excitation of specific polarization is zero. Further, the interband Berry connection between bands 1 and 3 shows irreducible Dirac strings connecting the $K$ and $K'$ points in the Brillouin zone, lines along which the interband Berry connection abruptly changes orientation. Our work establishes optical response as a powerful tool for characterizing geometrical and topological properties of band structure.
summary: This paper presents an experimental method to measure the interband Berry connection in an optical honeycomb lattice using lattice shaking. By observing interband excitations in ultracold fermionic atoms, the authors map out the geometric properties of the bands, including the discovery of Dirac strings. This establishes optical response as a robust tool for characterizing the topology of synthetic lattices.
main_problem: Direct experimental determination of the interband Berry connection and the geometric properties of Bloch bands in a crystalline-like structure.
main_result: The researchers successfully mapped the interband Berry connection in an optical honeycomb lattice, identifying transparency lines and irreducible Dirac strings in the connection between the first and third bands.
method: Using periodic modulation of the lattice position (lattice shaking) to simulate optical excitation and measuring the resulting interband population depletion via momentum-resolved absorption imaging.
model_or_system: A degenerate Fermi gas of 40K atoms in a 2D optical honeycomb lattice created by three intersecting 1064 nm laser beams.
key_observables: Interband excitation probability (fractional depletion), interband Berry connection components (Ax, Ay), and transparency lines.
important_parameters: Lattice depth V0 = 8.95 ER, shaking frequency 2pi x 3 kHz or 14.35 kHz, and temperature ~ 0.2 TF.
main_assumptions: The validity of first-order perturbation theory for describing lattice-shaking-induced excitations and the assumption that the excitation probability is real.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the band structure and lattice setup; Fig 3 displays measured magnitudes of the Berry connection and transparency lines; Fig 4 illustrates the orientation of the Berry connection and the presence of Dirac strings.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of measuring band geometry, describes the experimental setup of the honeycomb lattice, details the lattice-shaking method, presents the mapping of the Berry connection, and discusses the identification of topological features like Dirac strings.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a powerful technique to use optical-like responses in ultracold atoms to probe the quantum geometry and topological properties of band structures, which is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and synthetic gauge fields.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

For excitation from the ground band, the blue dashed line corresponds to the 3 kHz shaking frequency used for Fig. 2. Red and purple shaded regions correspond to frequency ranges to measure ⃗A13 and ⃗A14, respectively. Fig. 2. Polarization-dependent optical excitation be- tween the lowest two bands. a, Graphical representation of the matrix element. The direction of ⃗A21 is plotted along a circle around the Γ point. The direction of linear shaking along θPM is indicated by the gray arrow. Transparency at ⃗q occurs when ⃗A12 · ⃗ϵ(θPM) = 0. b, Angular positions ϕ of the transparency lines as a function of θPM. The positions are obtained by fitting a sum of four Gaussian functions to the angular integral of the excitation ratio within the annu- lar region enclosed in the purple dashed lines in panels d - f. Fig. 4. The orientation β of ⃗A14 and ⃗A13 in different gauges. a, b, β for ⃗A14 in a continuous gauge choice exhibits no phase jumps away from high-symmetry points. Unfilled circles in a indicate paths taken in b. The hexagonal inset shows the gauge map η(⃗q) used to evaluate β = arctan(sgn(R)|Ay|/|Ax|). White-filled circles in both figure and inset indicate corners of the Brillouin zone. c, d, By changing the gauge choice (see inset), lines of discontinuity in β are introduced, marked as white dashed lines; these are Dirac strings.
